dialogues.text: I want everything settled before the week's out — I don't sleep right with loose ends on the books. Here's what we still owe you. Forty. Take it.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. I've always run a clean operation. I'd like that to be the thing it's remembered for.
dialogues.no_response: It'll be here. I'm not going anywhere, and neither is what's owed. Come back for it.

dialogues.text: I heard from HR this morning. I'm not going to ask you a single question about it — that's not the kind of conversation this is. But take this. Forty.
dialogues.yes_response: You were one of the good ones. I don't hand that sentence out often. Don't let it go to your head.
dialogues.no_response: I'll hold it in the drawer. Come back for it when you're ready. The drawer doesn't forget.

dialogues.text: There's pay outstanding from last quarter — the bonus that got tangled in the accounting. I want it in your hand, not the company's. Forty.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. Consider it done and off my conscience both. I prefer my ledgers honest.
dialogues.no_response: I'll put it in an envelope with your name on the front. It is not going anywhere without you.

dialogues.text: You put in good work here. More of it than I ever let on to your face — that was a managing decision, and not my best one. Take the forty.
dialogues.yes_response: I should've said it while it could've changed how a Monday felt for you. That part's on me.
dialogues.no_response: Your call. But know it was earned, every dollar. I don't give money I don't mean.

dialogues.text: I'm squaring up every account that has your name near it. I don't leave things half-finished — never have, it's the one virtue I'm sure of. Forty.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. That's how I've always run things, and how I intend to keep running them.
dialogues.no_response: It's yours either way. I'll see to that personally. The decision's mine, not yours.

dialogues.text: I covered a thing on your behalf — quietly, last month, before it could land on your desk. I don't want it back. Just — take the forty and we're even.
dialogues.yes_response: Don't go making it sentimental on me. Just take it and let an old man feel useful for a minute.
dialogues.no_response: Stubborn. That's not always the virtue people sell it as. Come back for it when you're sensible.

dialogues.text: There's a thing nobody says enough in this line of work, so I'll say it once and we'll never mention it again. You mattered here. Take the forty.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. Now don't make me say anything else out loud. I've used up my whole year's allotment.
dialogues.no_response: The money doesn't expire. The sentiment underneath it doesn't either. Both keep.

dialogues.text: I've been doing this a long time. Long enough to feel it in a room when something's winding down — a contract, a quarter, a man. Forty. While I can hand it over.
dialogues.yes_response: Go. While you still can, go. That's not nothing — that's most things, when you tally it up.
dialogues.no_response: The offer doesn't close at five today. Whenever you're ready, the door and the drawer both open.

dialogues.text: Final pay period, all of it reconciled. I ran the numbers twice because I wanted them to be right for once, start to finish. Here's your forty.
dialogues.yes_response: Good luck out there. I mean that in the way that doesn't need a speech wrapped around it.
dialogues.no_response: Alright. I'll note it down. It doesn't disappear off the books just because you walked.

dialogues.text: Take this. And don't stand there telling me you're fine — I've signed off on enough timesheets to know a tired man when one's in front of me. Forty, and go see whoever you need to see.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. That's the right call. I wish I'd made more of those when the making of them was easy.
dialogues.no_response: Alright. But I hope you'll reconsider how you're spending your hours. That's the one account I can't settle for you.
